description: To perceive odor through the nose. 'Whinu' names the most ancient and evocative of senses—smell bypasses conscious processing to trigger memory and emotion directly. It detects the invisible signatures of things, reading the air for information sight cannot provide.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'whi' /ʍi/, the voiceless breath drawn in through the nose—air carrying scent molecules to receptors. The bright 'i' suggests the sharpness of olfactory perception, the clarity of scent detection. Then 'nu' resonates nasally, sound and sense meeting in the organ itself, the nose vibrating with what it has received.
grammatical_notes: 'mia thena whinu.' (I smell something.) The noun sense, the smell perceived, comes by the event-noun rule: 'welao whinu' (a pleasant scent).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Smell triggers memory and craving powerfully. Mindful attention to scent reveals how quickly pleasant or unpleasant feeling arises from contact.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Smell reads what eyes cannot see—smoke on the wind, rain coming, food gone bad, the health of person or animal. Ancient survival sense.

semantic_domains.cognition: Direct pathway to memory and emotion
